The victory opened Chihuahua to American occupation and was one of the most complete tactical successes of the war. The battle site on the Sacramento River valley north of Chihuahua City, Mexico, is marked by a monument and largely consists of open agricultural land. Period military artifacts have been found in the area during farming and construction activities.

name: Battle of San Pasqual
description: On December 6, 1846, Brigadier General Stephen Kearny's exhausted dragoons clashed with California Lancers under Andrés Pico at the Indian village of San Pasqual in the hills east of present-day San Diego, CA, in the bloodiest battle of the California campaign — the lancers' long lances proved deadly against the Americans' damp firearms, killing 21 dragoons. Kearny's surviving force was surrounded for two days before Kit Carson, Edward Fitzgerald Beale, and an Indian guide slipped through enemy lines to bring relief from San Diego. San Pasqual Battlefield State Historic Park near Escondido, CA, preserves a portion of the valley terrain with interpretive trails and a monument. The park prohibits metal detecting, but the surrounding San Pasqual Valley private farmland and equestrian properties have occasionally yielded period artifacts from the 1846 battle.

name: Battle of La Mesa
description: On January 9, 1847, American forces under Commodore Robert Stockton and General Stephen Kearny defeated the last organized Californio resistance at La Mesa near the San Gabriel River crossing (modern Montebello, CA), one day after a similar engagement at San Gabriel. The twin victories effectively ended the California campaign and led to the capitulation of Los Angeles. The battle site is now within the dense suburban landscape of the eastern San Gabriel Valley in Los Angeles County, with no surviving battlefield landscape. The Rancho era terrain of the Los Angeles basin saw extensive military activity in 1846-47, and period artifacts have occasionally been found during construction projects in the area.

name: Battle of Cerro Gordo Pass
description: On April 17-18, 1847, General Winfield Scott's Army executed a bold flanking movement discovered personally by Captain Robert E. Lee through the rugged mountains at Cerro Gordo, Veracruz, enabling American troops to attack Santa Anna's rear defenses and causing the entire Mexican defensive line to collapse. The victory cleared the mountain approaches to the Valley of Mexico and demonstrated the superior operational planning of Scott's staff, with Lee's reconnaissance proving decisive. The mountain pass between Veracruz and Jalapa in modern Veracruz state, Mexico, is accessible via Federal Highway 140, with a small monument near the site of the battle. The rugged terrain largely retains its 1847 appearance, and period artifacts including Mexican cannon balls and military hardware have been found in the area.

name: Battle of Fort Sumter
description: On April 12-13, 1861, Confederate forces under General P.G.T. Beauregard bombarded the Federal garrison at Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor, South Carolina, for 34 hours before Major Robert Anderson surrendered the island fort — the first shots of the Civil War. Miraculously, no one was killed in the bombardment itself (two Union soldiers died in an accidental explosion during the surrender salute), but the attack on a Federal installation electrified both North and South and triggered Lincoln's call for 75,000 volunteers. Fort Sumter National Monument is accessible by ferry from Charleston, SC, with the original fort walls still standing above the modern water level — the fort was built up with rubble as a gun platform during the long 1863-1865 siege. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national monument, but the Charleston Harbor bottom and surrounding barrier islands have been fruitful for underwater archaeological recovery of Civil War naval hardware.

name: First Battle of Bull Run (First Manassas)
description: On July 21, 1861, the first major land battle of the Civil War ended in a shocking Confederate victory when Thomas Jackson earned his 'Stonewall' nickname holding the Henry House Hill as the Union army of 35,000 broke and fled back toward Washington in a rout, dashing Northern hopes for a quick, easy war. Confederate General Beauregard and General Joe Johnston, combining their forces, overwhelmed the Federal army under McDowell with a flank attack that caused the Union line to collapse. Manassas National Battlefield Park in Prince William County, VA, preserves both the First and Second Manassas battlefields with miles of driving and walking tours, earthworks, and the restored Henry House. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Prince William County private land has yielded Minié balls, US and CS belt buckles, buttons, and artillery fragments from both 1861 and 1862 battles.

name: Battle of Wilson's Creek
description: On August 10, 1861, the first major Civil War battle west of the Mississippi was fought at Wilson's Creek, MO, where General Nathaniel Lyon led 5,400 Union soldiers in a two-column surprise attack on a Confederate force of 12,000 under General Ben McCulloch. Lyon was killed leading a charge — the first Union general killed in the war — and his outnumbered force was ultimately driven from the field, leaving southwestern Missouri contested ground for the rest of the war. Wilson's Creek National Battlefield near Republic, MO, preserves the rolling Ozark terrain with a 5-mile driving tour, walking trails, and the restored Ray House farm. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Christian and Greene County farmland has yielded Civil War Minié balls, buttons, US and CS buckles, and artillery fragments on private land.

name: Battle of Ball's Bluff
description: On October 21, 1861, a Federal reconnaissance across the Potomac River at Ball's Bluff near Leesburg, VA, turned into a disaster when Colonel Edward Baker (a close friend and former Senate colleague of Lincoln) led his command over the cliff-edged bluff and was slaughtered by Confederate forces, with Baker killed and hundreds of Union soldiers driven into the river. The defeat triggered Congress to create the Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War and led to the arrest of General Charles Stone. Ball's Bluff Battlefield Regional Park in Leesburg, VA, preserves the wooded bluff and a small National Cemetery — one of the smallest in the country — with the graves of those who drowned. Metal detecting is prohibited in the regional park, but the surrounding Loudoun County farmland and river corridor has yielded Civil War artifacts.

name: Battle of Belmont
description: On November 7, 1861, Brigadier General Ulysses Grant led 3,100 Union soldiers across the Mississippi River from Cairo, IL, to attack a Confederate camp at Belmont, MO — Grant's first Civil War combat command. Though Grant initially routed the Confederate camp, a Confederate counterattack cut off his force and he barely escaped back to his transports, learning harsh lessons about maintaining cohesion after success. The battlefield at Belmont, near Columbus, KY (across the river), is on private agricultural land in Mississippi County, MO, with a small state monument. The bottoms farmland along the Mississippi has produced Civil War Minié balls and camp debris from this early 1861 fight.

name: Battle of Carnifex Ferry
description: On September 10, 1861, Union forces under General William Rosecrans attacked a Confederate position commanded by General John Floyd at Carnifex Ferry on the Gauley River in western Virginia, driving Floyd from his entrenchments in a battle that helped secure the largely Unionist western Virginia counties that would become West Virginia in 1863. Floyd's nighttime retreat left behind camp equipment and supplies that the Union forces captured. Carnifex Ferry Battlefield State Park in Nicholas County, WV, preserves the high bluff above the Gauley River with earthwork traces, interpretive trails, and a museum in a restored farmhouse. Metal detecting is not permitted in West Virginia state parks, but the surrounding Nicholas County farmland along the river corridor has produced Civil War relics on private land.

name: Battle of Cheat Mountain
description: In September 1861, Confederate General Robert E. Lee's first independent field command ended in failure when his planned five-column attack on the Union position atop Cheat Mountain Summit in western Virginia never coordinated properly, with one column prematurely revealing its presence and the attack called off without a general engagement. The battle — fought in dense forest, rain, and cold — gave Lee his first nickname: 'Granny Lee' and 'Evacuating Lee' from critical Southern newspapers. Cheat Summit Fort, part of Monongahela National Forest, preserves the earthwork remains of the Union fortification in a remarkable forest setting along U.S. Route 250 in Pocahontas County, WV. The surrounding national forest and private lands saw extensive 1861 military operations, and the area is one of the more remote and historically intact Civil War mountain campaign zones in the country.

name: Battle of Rich Mountain
description: On July 11, 1861, General George B. McClellan's subordinate General William Rosecrans flanked a Confederate position at Rich Mountain, WV, capturing the mountain pass and precipitating the surrender of the main Confederate force under Colonel John Pegram — an early Federal victory that launched McClellan's national reputation and his appointment to command the Army of the Potomac. The campaign in western Virginia was the Union's first significant land success of the war. Rich Mountain Battlefield Civil War Site in Randolph County, WV, preserves a portion of the terrain with interpretive trails through the forested mountain landscape. The remote mountain setting has preserved much of the terrain, and private land in the surrounding area has yielded Civil War buttons, Minié balls, and camp artifacts from the 1861 operations.

name: First Battle of Lexington (Missouri)
description: In September 1861, Confederate General Sterling Price besieged and captured a Union garrison of 3,500 men under Colonel James Mulligan at the Missouri River town of Lexington, MO, in a three-day battle notable for the Confederate use of hemp bales soaked in the river as a rolling breastwork that advanced their siege lines without casualties. The fall of Lexington secured Price's control of western Missouri and boosted Confederate morale in the border state. Battle of Lexington State Historic Site preserves Anderson House (used as a field hospital), earthworks, and the battle site along the Missouri River bluffs. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but the broader Lafayette County private farmland along the Missouri River has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and camp debris from 1861 operations.

name: Battle of Hoke's Run
description: On July 2, 1861, a Federal column under General Robert Patterson skirmishing with Confederate forces under General Thomas Jackson at Falling Waters (Hoke's Run) near Martinsburg, WV, gave Jackson — still weeks before his famous 'Stonewall' nickname — his first engagement of the war. The skirmish delayed Patterson's pursuit and allowed Jackson's force to withdraw in good order toward Winchester. The battle site along Back Creek at Hoke's Run in Berkeley County, WV, is largely private farmland in the Shenandoah Valley. The Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia saw extensive military activity from 1861 through 1865, and the valley farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and buckles on private land with permission.

name: Battle of Fort Henry
description: On February 6, 1862, Flag Officer Andrew Foote's Union river gunboat fleet pounded Confederate Fort Henry on the Tennessee River into surrender before Grant's ground troops even arrived — the first time a U.S. Navy vessel had reduced a Confederate fort. The victory opened the Tennessee River as a highway deep into the Confederate heartland and set the stage for the larger victory at Fort Donelson days later, cementing Grant's rising reputation. The original site of Fort Henry is now beneath Kentucky Lake (the reservoir created by Kentucky Dam in 1944), a submerged Civil War fortification. The surrounding Stewart County, TN, area saw extensive military operations in early 1862, and private land in the region has yielded Civil War artifacts.

name: Battle of Fort Donelson
description: On February 11-16, 1862, Grant besieged Fort Donelson on the Cumberland River, repulsing a Confederate breakout attempt and forcing the surrender of over 12,000 Confederate troops — the largest surrender of soldiers in American history up to that point — earning him the nickname 'Unconditional Surrender Grant' that propelled him to national fame. The fall of Fort Donelson opened Nashville to Union occupation and effectively split the Confederacy's western defensive line. Fort Donelson National Battlefield near Dover, TN, preserves the earthworks, water batteries, the Dover Hotel (where the surrender occurred), and the national cemetery. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Stewart County private farmland has yielded Civil War Minié balls, CS and US belt plates, buttons, and artillery fragments from the intense February 1862 siege.

name: Battle of Pea Ridge (Elkhorn Tavern)
description: On March 7-8, 1862, the largest Civil War battle west of the Mississippi was fought at Pea Ridge in the Ozark Mountains of northwestern Arkansas, when Union General Samuel Curtis's 10,500-man Army of the Southwest defeated a Confederate force of over 16,000 under Van Dorn in a two-day battle that secured Missouri for the Union and is remarkable for involving three Confederate generals (McCulloch, McIntosh, and Hébert) either killed or captured. The battle effectively ended Confederate hopes of retaking Missouri. Pea Ridge National Military Park near Garfield, AR, preserves the battlefield with the reconstructed Elkhorn Tavern, driving tour, and excellent interpretive trails across the rolling Ozark terrain. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Benton County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and artillery fragments.

name: Battle of Shiloh (Pittsburg Landing)
description: On April 6-7, 1862, the Battle of Shiloh in Tennessee was the bloodiest engagement in American history to that point, with nearly 24,000 combined casualties over two days as Confederate General Albert Sidney Johnston's surprise attack nearly destroyed Grant's Army of the Tennessee on the first day before Johnston was killed and Union reinforcements under Buell turned the tide for a Union victory on the second. The battle shattered the comfortable illusion that the war would be short and shocked both North and South into the reality of what lay ahead. Shiloh National Military Park near Savannah, TN, preserves 4,200 acres of the battlefield with the distinctive 'Hornet's Nest' position, Bloody Pond, and hundreds of monuments. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Hardin County private land has yielded significant quantities of Civil War Minié balls, artillery shell fragments, US and CS buttons, belt plates, and camp debris.

name: Battle of Island Number Ten
description: In March-April 1862, a Union combined arms operation of gunboats and mortar boats besieged and captured Island No. 10 — a heavily fortified Confederate position in a bend of the Mississippi River on the Tennessee-Missouri border — using a remarkable canal cut through the Missouri swamps to flank the island's defenses. The fall of the island opened the river to Union traffic deep into the Confederacy and represented one of the most innovative amphibious operations of the war. The original island is now partially submerged and reconfigured by the shifting Mississippi River channel near New Madrid, MO, with no developed historic site. The surrounding river bottoms have yielded Civil War naval and military artifacts from the months-long siege operation.

name: Battle of Williamsburg
description: On May 5, 1862, as the Confederate army withdrew up the Virginia Peninsula toward Richmond, a Confederate rear guard under General James Longstreet fought a day-long delaying action against the pursuing Union Army of the Potomac at Williamsburg, VA, giving the main Confederate force time to escape northward. General Winfield Scott Hancock's spectacular attack on the Confederate left won him the 'Superb' sobriquet from McClellan. The battlefield is partially preserved at the Williamsburg battlefield sites within the growing James City and York County, VA, suburban area, with some earthworks visible. The broader Colonial/Peninsula Campaign corridor in the Virginia Tidewater has produced significant Civil War relics on private land, including Minié balls, US and CS buttons, and camp debris.

name: Battle of Fair Oaks (Seven Pines)
description: On May 31 - June 1, 1862, Confederate General Joseph Johnston attacked the Union Army's left wing that had crossed the Chickahominy River and was isolated by flooding at Fair Oaks Station and Seven Pines, VA. Johnston was severely wounded on the first day — an event that brought Robert E. Lee to command the Army of Northern Virginia — and the battle ended inconclusively with over 11,000 combined casualties. The battlefield is now within the Richmond metropolitan area in Henrico and Charles City counties, VA, largely developed but with a small marker at the Fair Oaks battlefield site. The Richmond and Chickahominy area has been exceptionally productive for relic hunters on private land, with Minié balls, artillery fragments, US and CS buttons, and belt plates documented from the Seven Days Battles area.

name: Battle of Mechanicsville (Beaver Dam Creek)
description: On June 26, 1862, Robert E. Lee launched his first offensive as commander of the Army of Northern Virginia by attacking the Union right flank at Beaver Dam Creek (Mechanicsville), VA, where Stonewall Jackson's force was expected to flank the position but failed to appear on time, causing frontal Confederate assaults to be repulsed with heavy losses. Despite the tactical failure, McClellan's reaction was to order a 'change of base' — what critics called a retreat — toward the James River. The battlefield area is partially preserved at the Richmond National Battlefield Park's Mechanicsville unit in Hanover County, VA. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park units, but the surrounding Hanover and Henrico County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the Seven Days campaign.

name: Battle of Gaines' Mill
description: On June 27, 1862, Robert E. Lee launched massed Confederate assaults against the Union position on Turkey Hill above Boatswain's Creek at Gaines' Mill, VA, in the largest of the Seven Days Battles — 57,000 Confederate troops against 35,000 Union defenders. The assault finally broke through at dusk when John Bell Hood's Texans and Law's Alabamians stormed the heights, forcing Fitz-John Porter's corps to retreat across the Chickahominy River. Richmond National Battlefield Park's Gaines' Mill unit preserves portions of the battlefield terrain in Hanover County, VA, with the restored Watt House serving as an interpretive stop. The surrounding private farmland in Hanover County has yielded exceptional quantities of Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, CS and US buttons, and belt plates from this intense engagement.

name: Battle of Savage's Station
description: On June 29, 1862, the Army of the Potomac fought a rear-guard action at Savage's Station on the Richmond and York River Railroad as it retreated toward the James River, leaving behind a field hospital of 2,500 wounded men who fell into Confederate hands — a humanitarian tragedy of the Seven Days campaign. Confederate General John Magruder's attack was repulsed, but the Union army continued its retreat. The battlefield in the White Oak Swamp area of Henrico County, VA, is now within the Richmond metropolitan area, largely developed. The White Oak Swamp corridor has produced Civil War relics during private land searches, including Minié balls, buttons, and accoutrements from the retreat.

name: Battle of Glendale (White Oak Swamp)
description: On June 30, 1862, the most dangerous day of the Seven Days Battles saw coordinated Confederate attacks converge on the Union army's retreating columns at Glendale and White Oak Swamp in Henrico County, VA, with A.P. Hill's and Longstreet's divisions driving into the Union flank — nearly cutting the army in two before the line was stabilized by desperate fighting. Stonewall Jackson inexplicably failed to press his attack at White Oak Swamp, allowing the Union army to escape what could have been annihilation. Richmond National Battlefield Park's Glendale/Malvern Hill unit preserves portions of the terrain, and private land in the surrounding area has produced significant Civil War artifact assemblages.

name: Battle of Malvern Hill
description: On July 1, 1862, the final battle of the Seven Days saw Robert E. Lee order repeated frontal assaults on the formidable Union artillery position atop Malvern Hill at Harrison's Landing, VA — massed Union cannon firing case shot and canister devastated the attacking Confederate brigades, killing or wounding 5,600 men in a battle D.H. Hill called 'not war, it was murder.' Despite the lopsided tactical victory, McClellan retreated to Harrison's Landing the following day, effectively ending the Peninsula Campaign. Richmond National Battlefield Park's Malvern Hill unit preserves the crest and approach terrain in Charles City County, VA, with excellent interpretive interpretation. The surrounding private farmland in Charles City and Henrico counties has yielded Civil War artillery shell fragments, Minié balls, canister rounds, and military equipment.

name: Battle of Cedar Mountain
description: On August 9, 1862, Confederate General Stonewall Jackson's corps attacked a Federal advance force under General Nathaniel Banks at Cedar Mountain (Slaughter Mountain) in Culpeper County, VA, in a hard-fought engagement where Banks's outnumbered men briefly broke Jackson's line before Confederate reinforcements restored the position. The battle was a prelude to the Second Manassas campaign and demonstrated the aggressive Confederate strategy that would characterize Lee's summer 1862 operations. Cedar Mountain Battlefield is partially preserved as a Virginia Civil War Trail site in Culpeper County, with interpretive markers and access to the mountain. The surrounding Culpeper County private farmland — one of the most intensively fought-over regions of the entire war — has produced large quantities of Civil War relics including Minié balls, artillery fragments, buttons, and belt plates.

name: Second Battle of Bull Run (Second Manassas)
description: On August 28-30, 1862, Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson executed one of the most brilliant strategic combinations in Civil War history at Second Manassas, with Jackson drawing the Union army into battle on the old First Manassas ground while Longstreet secretly massed his corps on the Union flank — Longstreet's devastating assault on August 30 crushed John Pope's Army of Virginia and sent it fleeing back toward Washington. Manassas National Battlefield Park preserves both battlefields with driving tours, earthworks, and interpretive walking trails across the rolling Virginia piedmont. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Prince William and Fauquier County private land has been exceptionally productive, yielding Minié balls, artillery fragments, US and CS belt plates, buttons, and enormous quantities of camp debris.

name: Battle of Chantilly (Ox Hill)
description: On September 1, 1862, a violent thunderstorm battle near Chantilly (Ox Hill) in Fairfax County, VA, saw Confederate forces under A.P. Hill attempt to cut off the retreating Union army, resulting in the deaths of two Union generals — Philip Kearny (killed accidentally riding into Confederate lines) and Isaac Stevens (shot through the head carrying a flag) — while the Confederates were repulsed. The battle effectively ended Lee's pursuit after Second Manassas. The Ox Hill (Chantilly) Battlefield Park in Fairfax County, VA, is a small urban park preserving a fragment of the original battlefield that survived development. Metal detecting is not permitted in the park, but the broader Fairfax County corridor has yielded Civil War relics during authorized private land investigations.

name: Battle of South Mountain
description: On September 14, 1862, three days before Antietam, Union forces forced three gaps through the South Mountain range in Maryland — Turner's Gap, Fox's Gap, and Crampton's Gap — against Confederate defenders, in a battle that revealed Lee's invasion plans (captured as 'Special Orders 191') and foreshadowed the catastrophic events at Antietam. The fighting at Fox's Gap was particularly brutal, with the death of Confederate General Samuel Garland and the wounding of several Union officers. South Mountain State Battlefield in Frederick and Washington counties, MD, preserves portions of the three gaps along the Appalachian Trail corridor, with earthwork traces still visible in the wooded terrain. Metal detecting is prohibited in the state park, but surrounding Maryland mountain private farmland has produced Civil War relics from the September 1862 campaign.

name: Battle of Harpers Ferry
description: On September 12-15, 1862, Stonewall Jackson besieged and captured the Union garrison at Harpers Ferry, WV, with 12,500 men — the largest single Union surrender of the war — after surrounding the town from the heights of Loudoun, Maryland, and Bolivar heights. The capture freed Lee's army to concentrate at Antietam, though it nearly cost him the campaign when McClellan moved faster than expected. Harpers Ferry National Historical Park preserves the historic armory town at the confluence of the Potomac and Shenandoah rivers, with extensive 19th-century buildings and interpretive programs. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding Jefferson County, WV, private farmland on the approaches to Harpers Ferry has yielded Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Antietam (Sharpsburg)
description: The Battle of Antietam on September 17, 1862, remains the bloodiest single day in American military history, with approximately 23,000 combined casualties as McClellan's Army of the Potomac attacked Lee's Army of Northern Virginia along Antietam Creek in Maryland, fighting through Cornfield, Bloody Lane, and the Lower Bridge. Despite tactical opportunities to destroy Lee's army against the Potomac, McClellan's cautious generalship allowed the Confederates to escape — but the Union's qualified 'victory' gave Lincoln the platform to issue the Emancipation Proclamation. Antietam National Battlefield near Sharpsburg, MD, is one of America's most intact Civil War battlefields, with miles of preserved farmland, the Sunken Road, Burnside Bridge, and the national cemetery. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Washington County private land has been remarkably productive — Minié balls, artillery fragments, US and CS belt plates, buttons, and canteen fragments have all been documented on private land in the campaign corridor.

name: Battle of Perryville
description: On October 8, 1862, the largest Civil War battle in Kentucky was fought at Perryville when Confederate General Braxton Bragg's invading army clashed with the Union Army of the Ohio under General Don Buell along Doctor's Creek in Boyle County. An acoustic shadow caused by atmospheric conditions meant that Buell and most of his army heard nothing of the fighting taking place nearby, delaying his response. Perryville Battlefield State Historic Site preserves approximately 1,000 acres of the original terrain with earthworks, the H.P. Bottom House, and a small museum. Metal detecting is prohibited in the state park, but surrounding Boyle County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, CS and US buttons, and belt plates from this overlooked but significant western theater battle.

name: Battle of Fredericksburg
description: On December 13, 1862, General Ambrose Burnside ordered suicidal frontal assaults against the strongly entrenched Confederate position on Marye's Heights above Fredericksburg, VA — wave after wave of Union soldiers fell in front of a stone wall lined with Southern riflemen, resulting in over 12,000 Union casualties in one of the war's most one-sided defeats. The Irish Brigade's assault on Marye's Heights, which reduced some regiments to skeletons, became one of the most celebrated acts of battlefield courage of the war. Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park preserves Marye's Heights, the Sunken Road, and the stone wall behind which Kershaw's South Carolinians stood. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Spotsylvania and Stafford County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, buttons, and belt plates in enormous quantities.

name: Battle of Stones River (Murfreesboro)
description: On December 31, 1862 through January 2, 1863, Braxton Bragg's Army of Tennessee launched a powerful dawn assault on the Union right at Stones River, routing two corps and nearly winning a decisive victory before the Union center held on the Nashville Pike. The battle — one of the war's bloodiest at 24,000 combined casualties — ended in a Union tactical victory when Bragg retreated after a final Confederate attack was repulsed with devastating losses. Stones River National Battlefield near Murfreesboro, TN, preserves the Slaughter Pen Farm, Hazen's Brigade Monument (the oldest intact Civil War monument), and the national cemetery. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Rutherford County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from both armies.

name: Battle of Prairie Grove
description: On December 7, 1862, Union forces under Generals Blunt and Herron fought a combined action at Prairie Grove, AR, repulsing Confederate General Thomas Hindman's attempt to destroy the two separate Union columns before they could unite — a Confederate plan that almost worked but was undone by Hindman's hesitation. The battle preserved Union control of northwestern Arkansas and the vital roads into Indian Territory. Prairie Grove Battlefield State Park in Prairie Grove, AR, preserves a remarkable 650 acres of original terrain with the Hindman Hall Museum, the Latta Barn complex, and excellent walking trails. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Washington County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and camp artifacts from this western Arkansas engagement.

name: Battle of Chickasaw Bayou
description: In late December 1862, William T. Sherman launched the first Union attempt to capture Vicksburg by landing 32,000 men in the Yazoo River bottomlands north of the city and assaulting the Confederate bluffs at Chickasaw Bayou, MS — the attacking Federals were cut down in the swampy lowlands by Confederate fire from the heights above, suffering 1,800 casualties to the defenders' 200. The defeat demonstrated the natural strength of Vicksburg's position and the difficulty of the task Grant would finally accomplish the following July. The battle site is now within the Vicksburg metropolitan area and National Military Park, with the bayou and bottomland terrain largely unchanged. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Warren County private land has produced Civil War relics from the extensive 1862-63 Vicksburg campaign operations.

name: Battle of Corinth
description: On October 3-4, 1862, Confederate Generals Van Dorn and Price attacked the Union garrison holding the vital railroad junction of Corinth, MS, with over 22,000 men — breaking through Union outer lines on the first day but being decisively repulsed in brutal street fighting on October 4 when the Union garrison under General Rosecrans held the inner works. The failed assault effectively ended Confederate offensive operations in northern Mississippi. Corinth Civil War Interpretive Center in Corinth, MS, is an NPS facility at Corinth National Cemetery that interprets this campaign and the related Siege of Corinth earlier in 1862. The surrounding Alcorn County private farmland and the broader Corinth area have produced Civil War relics from both the spring siege and the October battle, including Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Iuka
description: On September 19, 1862, the day before the Battle of Corinth, Union forces under General Rosecrans caught a portion of the Confederate army under General Sterling Price at Iuka, MS, in a battle intended as part of a two-pronged trap. Another acoustic shadow phenomenon prevented General Ord's force from hearing the battle and advancing, allowing Price to escape the trap though suffering significant losses. The town of Iuka in Tishomingo County, MS, has a small battlefield marker near the original engagement area; the terrain is largely private farmland and residential. The broader Iuka-Corinth corridor of northeastern Mississippi has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware on private land.

name: Battle of Fort Pulaski
description: On April 10-11, 1862, Union forces commanded by General David Hunter deployed experimental rifled artillery against Fort Pulaski — a masonry fort on Cockspur Island guarding the Savannah River in Georgia — and in 30 hours demonstrated that rifled guns had rendered brick-and-mortar fortifications obsolete, breaching the 11-foot-thick walls and forcing surrender. The battle revolutionized military engineering and signaled the end of masonry fortifications worldwide. Fort Pulaski National Monument on Tybee Island Road, GA, preserves the fort with the dramatic breach still visible in the southeast wall, one of the most evocative sights of Civil War military technology. Metal detecting is prohibited on the national monument, but the surrounding Savannah River barrier islands and coastal marshes have produced Civil War naval hardware and camp artifacts.

name: Battle of Hampton Roads
description: On March 8-9, 1862, the naval battles at Hampton Roads, VA, changed naval warfare forever: the Confederate ironclad CSS Virginia destroyed two Union wooden warships on March 8, and on March 9 met the Union's own ironclad USS Monitor in history's first clash between armored warships — a four-hour duel that ended inconclusively but demonstrated that the age of wooden warships was over. The Monitor sank in a storm off Cape Hatteras later that year, while the Virginia was scuttled to prevent capture. The Hampton Roads naval battlefield is within the waters of the James River and Chesapeake Bay off Newport News and Norfolk, VA; The Mariners' Museum in Newport News houses recovered Monitor artifacts and the original gun turret, recovered from 240 feet of water in 2002. The shipwrecks themselves are protected as archaeological sites.

name: Battle of Memphis
description: On June 6, 1862, a Union river fleet under Flag Officer Charles Davis attacked the Confederate River Defense Fleet above Memphis, TN, in a battle witnessed by thousands of Memphis citizens watching from the bluffs — within 90 minutes, all but one Confederate vessel had been captured or sunk, and the city surrendered. The fall of Memphis opened the upper Mississippi to Union control and made a rendezvous with Farragut's fleet from New Orleans only a matter of time. The city of Memphis is a major modern metropolis; the river battle site is within the Mississippi River channel adjacent to Memphis, TN. The Memphis riverfront and surrounding Shelby County area saw extensive Civil War occupation, and private land searches in outlying areas have yielded period military hardware.

name: Battle of Forts Jackson and St. Philip
description: On April 18-28, 1862, Flag Officer David Farragut's Union fleet fought its way past the twin Confederate forts guarding the mouth of the Mississippi River below New Orleans — Fort Jackson and Fort Philip — bombarding them for six days before running past them under fire in the predawn darkness of April 24 and proceeding to capture New Orleans without further resistance. The fall of New Orleans, the Confederacy's largest city and most important port, was one of the most consequential Union victories of the war. Fort Jackson (now a state historic site) and the adjacent area in Plaquemines Parish, LA, preserve the Confederate earthworks and original masonry of Fort Jackson. The lower Mississippi delta region has yielded Civil War naval hardware and fort artifacts, though the swampy terrain limits conventional metal detecting access.

name: Battle of Cross Keys
description: On June 8, 1862, during Stonewall Jackson's brilliant Shenandoah Valley Campaign, Confederate General Richard Ewell's division fought off a Federal assault at Cross Keys, VA, near Port Republic in Rockingham County, defeating General John Frémont's pursuing force and protecting Jackson's flank from destruction. The following day, Jackson defeated another Union force at Port Republic, ending the Valley Campaign. The Cross Keys battlefield in Rockingham County, VA, is a Virginia Civil War Trails site on private agricultural land, with interpretive markers along the road. The Shenandoah Valley saw some of the most intense and repeated military activity of the entire Civil War, and private land in Rockingham and Augusta counties has produced remarkable quantities of Civil War artifacts including Minié balls, buttons, and buckles.

name: Battle of Port Republic
description: On June 9, 1862, Stonewall Jackson fought the final battle of his Valley Campaign at Port Republic, VA, where he personally directed the attack on a Union force under General James Shields, routing them after fierce fighting around a coaling (charcoal-making facility). The twin victories at Cross Keys and Port Republic freed Jackson to join Lee in the Seven Days Battles, and completed one of the most remarkable military campaigns in American history. The Port Republic battlefield in Rockingham County, VA, is largely private farmland along the Shenandoah River, with Civil War Trails markers accessible from the road. The Valley Campaign corridor from Staunton to Strasburg has produced extraordinary quantities of Civil War relics on private land throughout the Shenandoah Valley.

name: First Battle of Winchester
description: In May 1862, during the Shenandoah Valley Campaign, Stonewall Jackson's forces drove the Union garrison under General Nathaniel Banks out of Winchester, VA — the first of three battles that would be fought in and around this key Valley town over the course of the war. Jackson's men swept northward through the Valley, threatening Washington and pulling troops away from McClellan's Peninsula Campaign. Winchester is a modern city in Frederick County, VA, and the battlefield terrain of the 1862 fight is largely within the urban and suburban environment. Shenandoah Valley battlefield protection organizations have worked to preserve portions of all three Winchester battles; private land in the surrounding countryside has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Front Royal
description: On May 23, 1862, Stonewall Jackson achieved complete tactical surprise at Front Royal, VA, when his forces — guided through the Blue Ridge by Belle Boyd's intelligence — routed a Union garrison under Colonel John Kenly and captured over 700 men, capturing enormous stores of supplies and forcing Nathaniel Banks to abandon Strasburg and flee northward. The attack at Front Royal triggered the chain of events that would lead to the battles at Winchester and ultimately Jackson's raid to the Potomac. The Front Royal area in Warren County, VA, has preserved some battlefield terrain, with Civil War Trails interpretive sites marking the route of the attack. The broader Shenandoah Valley private farmland in this area has produced significant Civil War artifact assemblages.

name: Battle of McDowell
description: On May 8, 1862, in the opening engagement of Stonewall Jackson's Valley Campaign, Confederate forces under General Edward Johnson clashed with a Union advance force under General Robert Milroy on Sitlington's Hill above the village of McDowell in Highland County, VA — the Federals actually attacked uphill to prevent the Confederates from bringing artillery to bear, inflicting heavier casualties than they suffered. The battle at 2,500 feet elevation in the Allegheny Mountains is among the more remote and intact Civil War battlefield landscapes in Virginia. McDowell Battlefield in Highland County, VA, is preserved by the Civil War Trust (American Battlefield Trust) and offers excellent hiking access to the Sitlington's Hill terrain. The remote Highland County area has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware, and the preserved battlefield is one of the most scenic and accessible of the smaller Virginia engagements.

name: Battle of Chancellorsville
description: The Battle of Chancellorsville in May 1863 is widely considered Robert E. Lee's masterpiece — outnumbered nearly two to one by General Joseph Hooker's 130,000-man Army of the Potomac, Lee divided his army three ways, sending Stonewall Jackson on an 11-mile flank march that crushed Hooker's exposed right flank at dusk on May 2 in a devastating surprise attack. The Confederate victory came at a terrible cost: Jackson was accidentally shot by his own men during a night reconnaissance and died eight days later. Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park's Chancellorsville unit preserves the Fairview and Hazel Grove artillery positions, the Chancellor House site, and Jackson's wounding location in the Wilderness. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Spotsylvania County private land has produced significant Civil War relics including Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from this intensely contested zone.

name: Battle of Fredericksburg (Second)
description: On May 3, 1863, during the Chancellorsville campaign, General John Sedgwick's Union Sixth Corps attacked Marye's Heights at Fredericksburg — the same position that had slaughtered thousands in December 1862 — and this time captured it quickly, then advanced toward Chancellorsville until stopped at Salem Church. The action, known as the Second Battle of Fredericksburg or Banks' Ford battle, was part of Hooker's broader but ultimately failed turning movement. The Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park preserves Marye's Heights, the Sunken Road, and Salem Church as part of the 1863 Chancellorsville campaign units. Private land in the broader Fredericksburg corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the intense 1862 and 1863 operations in this area.

name: Battle of Salem Church
description: On May 3-4, 1863, Sedgwick's Sixth Corps — having captured Marye's Heights — was stopped and then nearly surrounded at Salem Church, VA, when Confederate reinforcements arrived from Chancellorsville and counterattacked from both flanks. Sedgwick was forced to retreat back across the Rappahannock at Banks' Ford, abandoning the brief Union lodgment on the south bank. Salem Church, a brick antebellum church that served as a field hospital during the battle, still stands in Spotsylvania County and is part of the Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park. The surrounding private farmland in the church vicinity has produced Civil War Minié balls and military artifacts from the brief but fierce engagement.

name: Battle of Brandy Station
description: On June 9, 1863, the largest cavalry battle ever fought on American soil took place at Brandy Station, VA, when Union cavalry under Alfred Pleasonton launched a surprise raid on J.E.B. Stuart's Confederate cavalry corps that had been reviewed in grand parade the day before. Stuart was caught off guard, and the Union horsemen fought the Confederates to a tactical draw in a day-long battle across the rolling Culpeper County fields — demonstrating for the first time that the Union cavalry could match their Confederate counterparts. The Brandy Station Foundation has preserved a significant portion of Fleetwood Hill and the core battlefield in Culpeper County, VA, with a small museum. Metal detecting is prohibited on the preserved battlefield, but surrounding private farmland in Culpeper County has produced cavalry battle relics including carbine bullets, saber fragments, horse equipment, and buttons.

name: Battle of Aldie
description: On June 17, 1863, the opening engagement of the Loudoun Valley cavalry battles saw Union cavalry under General Judson Kilpatrick clash with Confederate cavalry under Colonel Thomas Munford at Aldie, VA, as Union horsemen tried to pierce the Blue Ridge screen and discover Lee's invasion route northward. The battle, fought in the narrow streets of the village and surrounding fields, was costly for the Federals. The town of Aldie in Loudoun County, VA, is a well-preserved historic village where the Aldie Mill historic site preserves the antebellum landscape of the battle. The surrounding Loudoun County piedmont farmland has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls on private land with permission.

name: Battle of Middleburg
description: On June 17-19, 1863, Union cavalry under General David Gregg fought a series of engagements at Middleburg, VA, as part of the Loudoun Valley cavalry battles that screened Lee's northward march toward Pennsylvania. The fighting around the historic Loudoun Valley village resulted in both sides suffering significant casualties without a clear decision. Middleburg, VA, is today an upscale equestrian community in Loudoun County with many of its antebellum buildings intact. The surrounding private horse country has produced Civil War cavalry relics on lands that have been lightly developed, making Loudoun County one of the more accessible private land detecting regions in Virginia.

name: Battle of Upperville
description: On June 21, 1863, the final engagement of the Loudoun Valley cavalry battles was fought at Upperville, VA, where Union cavalry finally pushed through the Blue Ridge gaps into the Shenandoah Valley — but found Lee's army of invasion already well on its way to Pennsylvania. The fighting at Upperville, won by the Confederates who retired in order, closed the prelude to Gettysburg. Upperville is a historic village in Fauquier County, VA, preserved in its antebellum appearance with stone walls and farmsteads. The surrounding Fauquier and Clarke county private farmland in the Virginia horse country has produced Civil War cavalry relics and camp artifacts on private property.

name: Battle of Gettysburg
description: The three-day Battle of Gettysburg in July 1863 was the bloodiest engagement of the Civil War, with over 50,000 combined casualties as Robert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia clashed with George Meade's Army of the Potomac across the rolling farmland, rocky hills, and orchards of southern Pennsylvania. Lee's failed assault on the third day — Pickett's Charge — broke the Confederate offensive momentum and marked the decisive turning point of the war. Gettysburg National Military Park is one of the most extensively preserved Civil War sites in America, with monuments, earthworks, stone walls, and thousands of acres of interpreted terrain. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited within the national park, but the surrounding Adams County farmland saw massive troop movements, camps, and hospital sites that have produced significant finds on private property with landowner permission.

name: Battle of Pickett's Charge
description: On July 3, 1863, the third day at Gettysburg culminated in the Confederate assault on the Union center at Cemetery Ridge — 12,500 men advancing nearly a mile across open fields under murderous artillery and infantry fire toward the Angle and Copse of Trees. Only a fraction reached the Union line, and their brief penetration was repulsed by the defenders; Pickett's division alone lost over 50% of its men. The charge site is the most-visited spot in Gettysburg National Military Park, preserved as open farmland almost exactly as it appeared in 1863. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the private farmland of Adams County in the approach corridors and flanking areas has produced exceptional quantities of Civil War relics including Minié balls, canister rounds, belt plates, and artillery fragments.

name: Siege of Vicksburg
description: From May 18 to July 4, 1863, General Grant besieged the Confederate fortress city of Vicksburg, MS, after his brilliant Vicksburg Campaign had cut off and surrounded General John Pemberton's 30,000-man garrison. The 47-day siege reduced the Confederate defenders and civilian population to near starvation before Pemberton surrendered on Independence Day — combined with the Gettysburg defeat, the fall of Vicksburg split the Confederacy along the Mississippi and was one of the war's decisive turning points. Vicksburg National Military Park preserves over 1,800 acres of siege lines, Confederate and Union earthworks, and the famous ironclad USS Cairo (raised from the Yazoo River and displayed in an outdoor pavilion). Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Warren County private land has produced Minié balls, artillery fragments, CS and US belt plates, buttons, and camp debris from the extensive siege operations.

name: Battle of Champion Hill
description: On May 16, 1863, the largest and most decisive engagement of Grant's Vicksburg Campaign was fought at Champion Hill (Baker's Creek), MS, where three Confederate divisions under Pemberton were driven from their defensive ridge position in a battle that effectively sealed Vicksburg's fate — preventing the Confederate army from escaping the trap Grant was closing around it. The victory at Champion Hill was called by Grant's chief engineer the 'decisive battle of the Vicksburg campaign.' The Champion Hill battlefield in Hinds County, MS, is largely private farmland with the Champion House (also called Sid Champion House) serving as the campaign's most recognizable landmark. The surrounding farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from this important but often overlooked engagement.

name: Battle of Big Black River Bridge
description: On May 17, 1863, the day after Champion Hill, Grant's army attacked the Confederate rear guard at the Big Black River railroad bridge in Warren County, MS, routing them in a brief engagement and capturing over 1,700 men — the fleeing Confederates abandoned artillery and equipment to escape across the bridge before destroying it. The defeat completed the Confederate retreat into the Vicksburg defenses. The battlefield at Big Black River Bridge is largely private farmland in Warren and Hinds counties, MS, with a Civil War Trails marker at the crossing. Private land in the area has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and accoutrements from the intense May 1863 operations.

name: Battle of Port Gibson
description: On May 1, 1863, the first major engagement of Grant's overland Vicksburg campaign was fought at Port Gibson, MS, after Union forces landed at Bruinsburg — the largest amphibious landing in American history before World War II. Grant's army fought its way through the dense bayou ridges against Confederate defenders under John Bowen, winning a victory that prompted the abandonment of Grand Gulf and opened the route to Vicksburg's rear. The Port Gibson battlefield in Claiborne County, MS, is partially preserved as a National Historic Landmark, with the Shaifer House and excellent interpretive signage. The surrounding Claiborne County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from this initial stage of Grant's masterful campaign.

name: Battle of Raymond
description: On May 12, 1863, Confederate General John Gregg attacked what he thought was a small Federal reconnaissance force near Raymond, MS — but encountered McPherson's entire 17th Corps in a sharp fight that was ultimately a Confederate tactical defeat. The surprise at Raymond forced Grant to alter his march route and strike Jackson before turning against Vicksburg, adding another dimension to his remarkable Vicksburg campaign. The Raymond battlefield in Hinds County, MS, is partially preserved at the Courthouse Square area in Raymond, with an NPS Civil War Trails interpretive stop. Surrounding Hinds County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the May 1863 campaign corridor.

name: Battle of Jackson (Mississippi)
description: On May 14, 1863, Grant's forces captured the Mississippi state capital at Jackson, MS, after a brief battle against Confederate defenders under Joseph Johnston, destroying the rail hub and manufacturing base that supported Vicksburg. Sherman's force briefly held the city before abandoning it to march on Vicksburg, and Johnston later returned to partially occupy the burned city. The battlefield at Jackson is within the modern capital city of Mississippi, largely developed, with the Old Capitol Museum and Mississippi Museum of Natural Science preserving antebellum buildings in the downtown core. Civil War artifacts have been found during construction projects in the Jackson metropolitan area, but conventional metal detecting opportunities are limited in the urban environment.

name: Battle of Chickamauga
description: The two-day Battle of Chickamauga on September 19-20, 1863 — fought in the thick forests and fields of northwestern Georgia — was the second-bloodiest battle of the Civil War, with nearly 35,000 combined casualties. On the second day, a miscommunication in the Union line created a gap just as General Longstreet's Confederate corps struck, shattering the Army of the Cumberland and sending it fleeing toward Chattanooga — with General George Thomas's heroic defense on Horseshoe Ridge earning him the name 'Rock of Chickamauga.' Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park — America's oldest military park — preserves the densely wooded battlefield terrain with the Visitor Center at Ft. Oglethorpe, GA, featuring one of the finest collections of Civil War artillery in existence. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Walker and Catoosa county private land has produced Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from this brutal engagement.

name: Battle of Chattanooga
description: In November 1863, the newly arrived Ulysses Grant directed a Union offensive that lifted the Confederate siege of Chattanooga, TN, culminating in one of the war's most dramatic moments: the unauthorized charge of the Army of the Cumberland up Missionary Ridge on November 25, where soldiers stormed the Confederate line in a fury born of their humiliation at Chickamauga. Bragg's army was routed from its seemingly impregnable positions, opening the gateway to Atlanta and marking the beginning of the end for Confederate forces in the western theater. Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park encompasses Lookout Mountain, Missionary Ridge, Orchard Knob, and other key positions in and around Chattanooga. Metal detecting is prohibited in all park units, but the surrounding Hamilton County, TN, private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Lookout Mountain
description: On November 24, 1863, Union forces under General Joseph Hooker swept Confederate defenders from the slopes of Lookout Mountain above Chattanooga, TN, in a battle fought in the clouds — literally, as a fog bank covered the upper mountain — that was romanticized as the 'Battle Above the Clouds' though much of the actual fighting occurred below the fog line. The capture of Lookout Mountain enabled the full-scale assault on Missionary Ridge the following day. Point Park on the Lookout Mountain summit is part of Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park, with dramatic views over the Tennessee River gorge and the city below. The surrounding Hamilton County, TN, residential and protected lands largely cover the battlefield approaches, but private land in outlying areas has produced Civil War artifacts.

name: Battle of Missionary Ridge
description: On November 25, 1863, the Army of the Cumberland spontaneously charged Missionary Ridge — the main Confederate defensive line above Chattanooga, TN — without orders, swarming up the steep ridge and sweeping the Confederate army from its seemingly impregnable position in one of the most dramatic moments of the entire Civil War. Grant and Thomas watched in disbelief as their soldiers charged unauthorized and succeeded, breaking Bragg's army and sending it retreating into Georgia. Missionary Ridge is today a residential ridge in Chattanooga, TN, with the national park preserving several unit positions including the Bragg Reservation and the tunnel area. Private land in the outer positions of the ridge and surrounding valley areas has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Fort Wagner
description: On July 18, 1863, the 54th Massachusetts Infantry — one of the North's first Black regiments, commanded by white Colonel Robert Gould Shaw — led the assault on Battery Wagner, a Confederate earthen fort guarding the entrance to Charleston Harbor, SC, in a frontal attack across a narrow beach that resulted in over 40% casualties and the death of Shaw. Though the assault failed to capture the fort, the 54th's courage under fire became a powerful argument for Black military service and is depicted in the film 'Glory.' The original site of Battery Wagner is now beneath the eroded shoreline of Morris Island, SC, with the spit of land continuing to erode into the sea. The Charleston Harbor area and Morris Island shoreline are periodically uncovered by erosion, and Civil War artifacts including artillery shells and military hardware have been found along the beach.

name: Battle of Chickamauga Creek
description: The Battle of Chickamauga on September 19-20, 1863, was fought across the farms and thickets along Chickamauga Creek and its tributaries in northwestern Georgia — a landscape of dense second-growth forest broken by small clearings, making unit coordination and visibility nearly impossible, contributing to the brutal, disjointed nature of the two-day battle. The creek itself, whose Cherokee name means 'River of Blood' (though this etymology is disputed), flows through the preserved Chickamauga battlefield to this day, still flanked by the same dense forest that confused both armies. Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park's Chickamauga battlefield unit in Fort Oglethorpe, GA, is an exceptionally well-preserved 5,500-acre Civil War landscape. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the park, but surrounding Walker County private farmland has produced Minié balls, artillery fragments, and CS/US military hardware.

name: Second Battle of Fort Fisher
description: Union capture of last major Confederate port, January 13-15, 1865, North Carolina.

name: Battle of Knoxville

name: Battle of Fort Sanders

name: Battle of Mine Run

name: Battle of Rappahannock Station

name: Battle of Port Hudson

name: Battle of Milliken's Bend

name: Battle of the Crater
